topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. "Due to unforeseen financial restructuring, the university will be ceasing operations at the end of the fiscal year." I reread the words three times, my fingers trembling slightly over the screen. It was impossible. Four years. All those late-night study sessions fueled by cheap coffee, the shared triumphs in the library, the entire structure of my young adulthood—all dissolving into a bureaucratic footnote. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair back with a loud scrape that echoed in the suddenly sterile dorm room. My jaw tightened, a hard, unyielding line forming where my mouth had been. I walked to the window, staring out at the campus quad, now seeming impossibly vibrant and alive, mocking the impending silence.

[story 2]

"So, this is it?" I asked, my voice dangerously level, leaning across the mahogany table toward the dean. I didn't raise my voice, which somehow made it sound colder, sharper. I watched the polished veneer of the administrator’s composure crack just slightly when I laid out my disappointment. My hands clenched into fists beneath the table, the knuckles turning white. I felt a strange, hot flush rising up my neck, a sensation entirely disproportionate to the news itself. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but I kept my tone measured, a practiced calm masking the churning frustration inside. "After everything I’ve invested—my time, my ambition—this is how you treat us?"

[story 3]

I kept staring at the acceptance letter I’d framed on my desk, the embossed seal suddenly feeling like a cruel joke. The official notice confirming the closure was tucked beneath it, stark white against the heavy cardstock. A slow, burning sensation started low in my stomach, radiating outward. I picked up the notice, my movements stiff and deliberate. I didn't cry; tears felt too weak for the magnitude of this disappointment. Instead, I smoothed the paper flat on the desk, the sound of the paper rustling sounding like tearing fabric. I pushed the chair back, the scrape this time laced with a deliberate, aggressive force. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the word tasting like ash on my tongue. I needed to feel something, anything, that matched the sheer scale of this letdown.
